planting.note: Set dormant crowns in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant crowns so the soil line sits right at the base of the buds, never burying the growing point. Pinch off the first season's flowers to build strong roots and runners, and mulch with straw before winter. The main crop of bright red, firm berries ripens all at once in early summer.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in well-drained, fertile soil.

directions: Space plants along a matted row and keep the crown level with the surrounding soil when planting. Remove blossoms the first year and allow runners to fill in the bed. Prized for flavor, its deep-red berries are among the earliest to ripen each June.

planting.note: Set out crowns in early spring for a crop the following year.

directions: Plant with the midpoint of the crown at soil level and firm the roots in gently. This vigorous, disease-resistant variety sends out many runners, so thin them to keep the bed productive. Large, glossy berries with mild sweet flavor ripen in mid-season.

planting.note: Plant dormant crowns in spring once frost danger eases.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and water well to settle the roots. A late-mid-season favorite, it rewards good care with large, firm, glossy berries. Keep beds weeded and renewed each year for the best yields.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in loose, fertile ground.

directions: Tuck crowns in with the growing tip just above the soil surface. This old, reliable jam variety produces heavy runners and softer, intensely flavored fruit. The late-season berries are small to medium and perfect for preserves.

planting.note: Set crowns out in early spring for cold-climate reliability.

directions: Plant crowns level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed. A hardy Canadian variety, it produces large berries over a spread-out ripening window. Mulch heavily for winter in the coldest zones.

planting.note: In mild regions set plugs in fall for a spring crop; elsewhere plant in early spring.

directions: Plant on raised, plastic-mulched beds in warm regions with the crown sitting proud of the soil. A leading commercial berry for mild winters, it yields big, wedge-shaped, sweet fruit. Harvest frequently once fruit colors up fully in spring.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; very cold-tolerant.

directions: Set crowns at soil level in a fertile, well-drained bed and remove first-year flowers. An early variety with good winter hardiness, it produces large, light-red berries. Renew the planting every few years to sustain vigor.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant crowns level with the surface and keep the bed consistently moist. Known for exceptionally large berries, this late-mid variety benefits from steady watering during fruiting. Pick when the whole berry has turned red for best size.

planting.note: Set crowns out in early spring for a following-year crop.

directions: Plant crowns with the tip just above the soil and firm gently. A productive mid-season variety, it bears firm, sweet berries that hold well after picking. Thin runners to keep the row from crowding.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; tolerant of a range of soils.

directions: Set crowns at soil level in almost any well-drained ground, as this variety is famously adaptable. Its drought tolerance and disease resistance make it forgiving for beginners. Large, tart-sweet berries ripen over a long mid-season window.

planting.note: Plant plugs in autumn on raised beds in mild-winter areas.

directions: Grow on plastic-mulched raised beds with the crown set above the soil to prevent rot. A heavy-yielding commercial berry for warm climates, it produces large, firm, deep-red fruit. Harvest often through the spring flush.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring, or fall in mild coastal areas.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in a warm, sunny spot. This early California variety fruits over an unusually long period in mild regions. Soft, richly flavored berries are best eaten soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level and keep the bed evenly watered. A French mid-season variety, it produces large, conical, aromatic berries with excellent flavor. Provide steady moisture to keep fruit sizing up.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring, well suited to the Pacific Northwest.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in a maritime climate bed. A soft, sweet processing variety, it is prized for jam and freezing. Pick promptly, as the tender berries do not hold long once ripe.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in cool, moist coastal soil.

directions: Plant crowns with the tip at soil level in a rich, well-drained bed. A Northwest processing variety, it holds color well when frozen. Harvest the medium-sized, deep-red berries when uniformly ripe.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a mild-winter maritime bed.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and keep the planting weed-free. A Northwest fresh-eating variety with good virus tolerance, it bears sweet, medium berries. Remove first-year blooms to build strong plants.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for a very early crop.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in a fertile, sunny bed. This vigorous early variety leafs out and blooms ahead of most, so protect early flowers from late frost. Firm, flavorful berries open the strawberry season.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in cold-winter regions.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in a well-drained northern bed. An early variety with a distinctive aromatic flavor, it is well adapted to the Northeast. Mulch for winter protection in the coldest zones.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; strong disease resistance.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in average garden soil. Valued for resistance to root diseases, it performs where other varieties struggle. Large, firm mid-season berries ripen over several weeks.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring for two crops the same season.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and remove flowers only through the first month. This popular everbearer produces a heavy early-summer crop and a second flush in fall. Keep well watered to sustain fruiting through the season.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; fruits on both plants and runners.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in rich, moist soil. This everbearer sets fruit even on unrooted runners, making it good for hanging baskets. Large, soft berries appear from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; extremely cold-hardy.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed. Bred for the northern plains, it survives hard winters with only light mulch. Sweet, aromatic berries come in an early and a late flush.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; drought- and cold-tolerant.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in a bed that stays well drained. Carrying wild strawberry ancestry, it tolerates heat, cold, and drought better than most. Soft, deeply flavored berries ripen from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for continuous fruiting through the season.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and pinch flowers for the first six weeks to establish roots. As a day-neutral, it fruits steadily regardless of day length from early summer to frost. Large, firm, conical berries have excellent sweet flavor and store well.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; tolerates warm summers well.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level and remove early flowers to build the plant. A heat-tolerant day-neutral, it produces large, glossy berries in repeated flushes. Harvest frequently to keep new fruit setting through the season.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; good for beds and containers.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and pinch blooms for the first month. This compact day-neutral bears small to medium, intensely sweet berries all season. Its disease resistance and modest runners suit tight spaces.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; disease-resistant day-neutral.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level and keep evenly watered for continuous fruiting. Resistant to red stele and verticillium wilt, it is dependable in problem soils. Firm, tart-sweet berries come in flushes from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; heavy-yielding day-neutral.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil in a fertile, well-drained bed. This vigorous day-neutral produces very large, firm berries over a long season. Feed lightly through summer to support the continuous crop.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; strong disease tolerance.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level and pinch early flowers to establish. A robust day-neutral, it yields large, firm, well-flavored berries with fewer disease problems. Pick regularly to encourage steady fruiting.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; high-yielding in warm climates.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and water consistently through fruiting. This productive day-neutral bears large, sweet berries with a lingering aftertaste. It thrives in mild-winter regions with a long growing season.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for heavy early production.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed. A high-yielding day-neutral, it produces large, uniform, mild-sweet berries. Harvest often to sustain the long fruiting run.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; heat-tolerant day-neutral.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and keep steadily watered. This day-neutral keeps setting fruit through summer heat that stalls other varieties. Medium-large, firm berries ripen from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; prized French dessert variety.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in rich, well-drained soil. Bred to capture the aroma of wild strawberries, this day-neutral rewards with intensely perfumed, sweet berries. Fruit is small to medium and best enjoyed fresh at peak ripeness.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds and press them into moist mix without covering, as light aids germination. Grow on and set plants out in dappled shade to full sun. This runner-forming wild species bears small, fragrant red berries over a long season and self-sows readily.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; transplant seedlings after the last frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface of moist mix and leave uncovered for light. This runnerless alpine forms tidy clumps ideal for edging and containers. It produces small, aromatic red berries continuously from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant after danger of frost passes.

directions: Surface-sow seed on moist mix and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. A clump-forming alpine that spreads by seed rather than runners, it suits borders and pots. Tiny, deeply flavored berries ripen steadily through summer and fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for transplanting after frost.

directions: Press seed lightly onto moist mix and leave exposed to light to germinate. This old German alpine forms neat runnerless clumps and tolerates part shade. It yields elongated, richly aromatic red berries from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and move seedlings out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it moist and lit until germination. This runnerless alpine ripens to a pale cream-yellow that birds tend to overlook. The sweet, pineapple-scented berries carry from summer well into autumn.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Chill seed for several weeks, then surface-sow on moist mix for light. A vigorous native groundcover, it spreads by runners to form a low mat. Small, intensely sweet red berries ripen in late spring and feed wildlife.

planting.note: Set dormant bare-root canes in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant canes so the roots sit just below the surface and firm the soil around the crown. This everbearing type fruits on first-year canes in fall and on the same canes again the following summer, so you can either cut everything to the ground in late winter for one large fall crop or leave floricanes for two lighter pickings. Harvest when berries slip easily from the core and glow a deep red.

planting.note: Plant this hardy heirloom in early spring while canes are still dormant.

directions: Space canes generously and trellis them to keep the tall summer-bearing growth upright. This old, very cold-tolerant variety fruits in midsummer on second-year canes, so prune out spent floricanes after harvest each year. Pick the round, medium-red berries once they release with a gentle tug.

planting.note: Set out dormant canes in early spring in a well-drained, sunny bed.

directions: Plant bare-root stock and water in well, keeping the crown near the soil surface. Extremely winter-hardy, this summer-bearing variety yields aromatic dark-red berries on floricanes in early to mid summer. Remove the fruited canes at ground level once picking finishes to make room for new growth.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; this variety tolerates a wide climate range.

directions: Set canes in a sunny row with good air movement to reduce disease. This vigorous, nearly spineless summer-bearer produces firm bright-red fruit that holds well after picking. Thin new canes to a manageable hedge and remove spent floricanes each fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root canes as soon as soil is workable in spring.

directions: Firm the soil around the roots and mulch to keep them cool and moist. A hardy summer-bearing variety, it ripens bright-red, sweet berries in early to mid summer on floricanes. Cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest and tie in the new season's growth.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set nearly thornless canes into loose soil and water regularly through the first season. This summer-bearing variety yields large, bright-red, mild berries that are easy to pick thanks to its smooth stems. Prune out old floricanes after fruiting and support the tall new canes on wire.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring, ideally in a mild maritime climate.

directions: Train the vigorous canes onto a two-wire trellis and keep the base mulched. A classic Pacific Northwest summer-bearer, it produces large, dark-red, slightly tart berries prized for freezing and jam. Harvest in early to mid summer and remove spent floricanes afterward.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring where summers are not extreme.

directions: Give this variety a sturdy trellis, as its long fruiting laterals can be heavy with berries. It produces large, glossy, sweet red fruit over an unusually long summer picking season. Remove fruited canes each year and thin new ones to keep rows open and productive.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; tolerant of heavier, wetter soils.

directions: This summer-bearer handles damp ground better than most, so it suits gardens with less-than-perfect drainage. Trellis the tall canes and expect very large, firm, flavorful red berries in mid summer. Cut out spent floricanes after the crop and keep new canes tied in.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: A leading commercial summer-bearer, Meeker forms long canes that need good trellis support. It ripens abundant, sweet, deep-red berries in mid summer that freeze and process well. Remove the old fruited canes after harvest and thin the new growth.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for an early summer crop.

directions: Set canes into rich soil and support them on wire as they lengthen. One of the earlier summer-bearing types, it yields large, firm, glossy red berries with good shelf life. Prune out fruited floricanes and tie in the current season's canes for next year.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for the season's earliest crop.

directions: This everbearing variety gives a small fall crop on new canes and the earliest summer crop of any raspberry the next year. Plant in a sunny, well-drained row and mulch to conserve moisture. Pick the medium-sized, firm red berries as soon as they part easily from the plant.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for a late-summer harvest.

directions: One of the latest summer-bearing raspberries, Encore extends the season with large, sweet, firm red berries. Grow the sturdy, nearly spineless canes on a simple trellis in full sun. Remove the spent floricanes after fruiting and thin the new canes each year.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for a first-year fall crop.

directions: This vigorous primocane variety fruits on new canes in late summer and fall, so you can mow the whole planting to the ground each winter for easy management. Plant in a warm, sunny bed and keep evenly watered as fruit sets. Harvest the large, richly flavored red berries when they slip free of the core.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for fall fruiting the same year.

directions: A widely grown primocane type, Autumn Bliss carries a heavy late-summer to autumn crop on sturdy, self-supporting canes. Cut all canes to ground level in late winter to concentrate the following year's harvest. Pick the large, deep-red berries once they detach with a light pull.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for a long autumn harvest.

directions: Polka is a productive primocane variety that begins fruiting in late summer and continues heavily into fall. Grow it in full sun and cut the canes to the ground each winter for a single strong crop. Harvest the large, firm, sweet red berries as they ripen.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; this variety is spine-free.

directions: A thornless primocane raspberry, Joan J is easy to pick and fruits from late summer into autumn on new canes. Plant in full sun and mow the canes down in late winter for a concentrated fall crop. Gather the large, soft, sweet berries frequently once ripening begins.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for a very late fall crop.

directions: This primocane variety ripens later than most, stretching the raspberry season deep into autumn where frosts hold off. Grow it in full sun and cut the planting to the ground in winter for one heavy crop. Pick the medium-sized, firm, sweet red berries as they loosen.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: A popular spine-free summer-bearer, Glen Ample produces heavy yields of large, firm, sweet red berries in mid summer. Train the tall canes onto wires and keep the row well weeded. Remove fruited floricanes after harvest and tie in the new canes for next season.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring, kept apart from red raspberries.

directions: Jewel is a hardy, productive black raspberry that fruits on second-year canes in early summer. Tip-prune the arching primocanes in summer to force branching, then thin the laterals in late winter. Harvest the glossy, sweet-tart black berries when they pull free cleanly.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring, away from red raspberry beds.

directions: A vigorous summer-bearing black raspberry, Bristol yields large, firm, richly flavored berries in early summer. Pinch the growing tips of new canes to encourage sturdy fruiting laterals. Cut out the fruited canes after harvest and pick berries when fully black and glossy.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in full sun with good airflow.

directions: This long-grown heirloom black raspberry bears sweet, seedy berries on floricanes in early summer. Tip the primocanes in summer and thin the resulting laterals during the dormant season. Harvest daily during the short ripening window, taking only fully colored fruit.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for a late black-raspberry crop.

directions: Mac Black is a late-ripening summer-bearing black raspberry that stretches the season past most other varieties. Tip-prune new canes in summer and shorten the laterals in late winter for the best fruit. Pick the firm, sweet black berries once they release easily.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring, favored in mild-summer regions.

directions: A standard processing black raspberry, Munger produces glossy, firm berries with strong flavor in early to mid summer. Pinch the primocanes to build a branched framework of fruiting wood. Remove spent floricanes after harvest and gather berries when uniformly black.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; noted for heat and drought tolerance.

directions: Blackhawk is a sturdy, adaptable black raspberry that holds up better than most in heat and dry spells. It fruits on floricanes in mid summer, producing large, firm, flavorful berries. Tip the new canes in summer and thin the laterals in winter for reliable yields.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; a golden sport of Heritage.

directions: A yellow-fruited version of Heritage, Kiwigold is everbearing and produces sweet golden berries blushed with pink. Grow it in full sun and manage it exactly like a red everbearer, either mowing for a fall crop or keeping floricanes for two. Harvest gently as the soft berries loosen from the plant.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; very hardy summer-bearer.

directions: Honey Queen is a cold-hardy summer-bearing yellow raspberry with soft, very sweet honey-flavored berries. Fruiting on floricanes in mid summer, it rewards gentle daily picking since the fruit is delicate. Remove spent canes after harvest and tie in the new growth for the following year.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; a red-black hybrid raspberry.

directions: Royalty is a vigorous purple raspberry, a cross between red and black types, bearing large reddish-purple berries in mid summer. Tip-prune the tall new canes to encourage branching and support them well. Pick berries firm and red for cooking, or fully purple and soft for the sweetest fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; a purple red-black hybrid.

directions: Brandywine is a tall purple raspberry producing large, tart reddish-purple berries that excel in jams and pies. Fruiting on floricanes in mid to late summer, it benefits from summer tipping and a strong trellis. Harvest when the berries turn full purple and pull away easily.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring; a compact thornless hybrid.

directions: Glencoe is a nearly thornless, compact purple raspberry well suited to smaller gardens and containers. It bears sweet, dark-purple berries on floricanes in summer with an unusually spine-free, easy-to-pick habit. Trim spent canes after harvest and keep new canes tied to a short support.

planting.note: Usually planted as canes in spring, though seed will germinate after cold stratification.

directions: The wild red raspberry species forms a suckering thicket of biennial canes that fruit in their second year. Plant canes in a sunny, well-drained spot and give the spreading roots room or a barrier to contain them. Harvest the fragrant red summer berries when they slip cleanly from the core, and cut out fruited canes each fall.

planting.note: Set dormant plants in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant crowns at the depth they grew in the nursery and tie the semi-erect canes to a two-wire trellis as they lengthen. Cut out canes that fruited right after harvest, leaving new green canes for next year. Large, glossy-black berries ripen over several weeks in mid to late summer and pull free easily when fully sweet.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while still dormant.

directions: Space plants along a sturdy trellis and train the long semi-erect canes horizontally to spread the fruiting wood. This cultivar tolerates cold better than most, so mulch the roots and let the canes ripen a heavy late crop. Harvest firm, mildly tart berries from late summer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant dormant erect canes in early spring.

directions: Set erect plants in a row and tip-prune the primocanes at about waist height in summer to force fruiting side branches. Remove spent floricanes at ground level after the harvest. Sweet, firm berries with strong flavor ripen in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as growth begins.

directions: Plant these self-supporting erect canes and pinch the growing tips in early summer to build a stockier, branching plant. Thin to a few strong canes per crown and remove old wood after fruiting. Very large berries ripen over a concentrated window in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Set dormant plants out in early spring.

directions: Grow these erect canes without a trellis, tipping the primocanes in summer to encourage branching. One of the earliest-ripening thornless types, so protect early bloom from late frost. Small-seeded, sweet berries come in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Plant erect canes in full sun and keep the row narrow by removing suckers that stray outside it. Tip the new canes in summer and cut fruited canes to the ground afterward. Firm, sweet berries ripen over a long stretch of early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: These semi-erect canes benefit from a low trellis or stake since they arch under a heavy load. Cut out fruited wood each year and keep the base tidy of suckers. Long, cylindrical berries ripen early and hold their shape well when picked.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as the soil warms.

directions: Set erect plants in a well-drained row and tip the primocanes in summer for a bushier habit. Remove the canes that just fruited to make room for next season's growth. Prized for dessert-quality flavor, the medium berries ripen through mid summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a first-year late crop.

directions: This thornless primocane type fruits on first-year canes, so it can bear a late crop the season it is planted. In warm regions you can also mow all canes down in winter and harvest only the late-summer primocane crop. Very large, sweet berries ripen from late summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; expect a fall primocane crop.

directions: These thorny erect canes carry both a summer floricane crop and a fall primocane crop, so prune to suit which you want. Tip the new canes in summer to build branching and firmness. Shippable, firm berries ripen in summer and again in early fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a first-year fall crop.

directions: A thornless primocane-fruiting cultivar that bears firm berries suited to handling and storage. Grow it for a double crop or cut canes to the ground for a single clean fall harvest. Sweet, uniform berries ripen from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Train the vigorous semi-erect canes onto a trellis to keep the heavy crop off the ground. Remove fruited canes after harvest and thin new canes to the strongest few. Large, tangy-sweet berries ripen in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: This popular Scottish selection produces stout semi-erect canes that need trellis support. Tie in new canes through the season and cut out the old fruited ones each winter. Firm, sweet-flavored berries ripen from mid summer onward.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: An early-ripening thornless type with a compact, manageable habit for smaller gardens. Fan the canes onto wires and remove spent wood after the crop finishes. Sweet berries begin ripening in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as growth resumes.

directions: One of the original thornless cultivars, it makes long trailing-to-semi-erect canes that must be trellised. Train the current year's canes aside from the fruiting ones to simplify pruning. Tart-sweet berries ripen late in the season.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Grow these vigorous semi-erect canes on a trellis and space plants generously to allow airflow. Cut fruited canes at the base after harvest and tie in the replacements. Sweet, firm berries hold up well and ripen in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as the soil warms.

directions: Set thorny erect canes in a sunny row and keep suckers thinned to a narrow hedge. Tip the primocanes in summer and clear fruited canes afterward. Large berries ripen over a long early-to-mid-summer window.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: A productive thorny erect cultivar valued for heavy yields of soft, sweet fruit. Tip the new canes for branching and cut fruited canes to the ground each year. Berries ripen over a long stretch of summer and are best used soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: A cold-hardy thorny erect cultivar suited to northern gardens. Grow the upright canes with light staking and remove old wood after harvest. Tangy berries ripen across an extended mid-summer season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; this trailing type prefers mild-winter regions.

directions: This classic Oregon trailing blackberry needs a two-wire trellis, with the long canes woven along the wires. Separate the current-year canes from the fruiting canes to make winter pruning easier. Intensely flavored, tender berries ripen in early to mid summer and are prized for pies and jam.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a mild, sheltered spot.

directions: Train the long trailing canes onto sturdy wires and keep the crown mulched. Tie in new canes during summer and remove the fruited ones after harvest. Large, wine-red-to-purple berries with a rich tart-sweet flavor ripen in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sheltered site.

directions: A spineless form of the boysenberry with the same deep flavor and easier handling. Weave the trailing canes onto a trellis and prune out spent wood each year. Soft, dark berries ripen in early summer and bruise easily, so pick gently.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: This blackberry-raspberry cross makes long trailing canes that should be fanned onto wires. Keep new canes to one side of the fruiting wood for tidy pruning and cut fruited canes out after harvest. Long, cone-shaped red-purple berries with an aromatic tart flavor ripen in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sheltered location.

directions: An old blackberry-raspberry hybrid with vigorous trailing canes that need firm support. Separate this year's canes from the fruiting ones along the trellis and remove old wood yearly. Long, dark-red tart berries ripen in early to mid summer and are excellent cooked.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a mild spot.

directions: A spineless loganberry that is far easier to train and harvest than the thorny form. Weave the long canes onto horizontal wires and prune fruited canes at the base. Tart red berries ripen in early to mid summer for jams, juice, and baking.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: A trailing hybrid similar to the boysenberry, with long canes best grown on wires. Tie in the new canes over summer and remove fruited wood after the crop. Sweet, dark-purple berries ripen in early summer and are good fresh or preserved.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sheltered site.

directions: An Australian trailing hybrid with early ripening and heavy crops. Support the vigorous canes on wires and remove spent fruited canes each year. Large, dark berries with a rich tart flavor ripen in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: A hardy tayberry relative bred in Scotland for cooler, wetter climates. Train the upright-trailing canes onto wires and cut out old wood after fruiting. Tart red-purple berries ripen in mid summer and are best cooked or preserved.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in warm-region gardens.

directions: An early dewberry-raspberry hybrid developed in Texas for hot climates. Support the trailing canes on wires and remove fruited canes after harvest. Soft, tart-sweet berries ripen in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Set plants or divisions in early spring; seed needs cold stratification and germinates slowly.

directions: This native eastern North American species forms thorny, arching thickets and spreads by suckers. Give it room or contain the roots, and thin old canes to keep it productive. Small, sweet-tart black berries ripen in mid to late summer and feed both people and wildlife.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted tips in early spring.

directions: This wild-type bramble is a variable species complex behind many garden cultivars. Its arching canes root at the tips, so manage spread and thin the oldest wood yearly. Sweet-tart black berries ripen from mid summer into early fall.

planting.note: Set dormant bare-root plants in early spring as soon as the soil can be worked.

directions: Plant with the crown an inch or two below the old soil line and cut the stems back hard the first spring to build a strong framework. Mulch generously and keep the shallow roots evenly moist through fruit set. Harvest whole strigs when the berries color bright red and taste tart-sweet, usually in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant while dormant; blooms early, so choose a site sheltered from late frost.

directions: Firm the soil around the roots and water in well, then mulch to keep the crown cool. Because it flowers early and ripens early, protect the buds if a hard frost threatens. Gather the deep red berries as soon as the strigs fill out, typically among the first currants of the season.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant slightly deep and prune out the oldest canes each winter to keep young wood productive. Feed with a balanced fertilizer in spring and water steadily during dry spells. Harvest the large, cherry-red berries when fully colored in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; this old American cultivar is vigorous and reliable.

directions: Bury the crown a couple of inches to encourage new canes from the base and mulch well. Renew the bush by removing wood older than three years each dormant season. Pick the bright red strigs when fully ripe; the fruit holds on the bush and resists dropping.

planting.note: Plant while dormant; a very late-ripening variety that extends the season.

directions: Set the plant firmly, mulch the root zone, and train to an upright open shape for easy picking. Its late season means the crop follows most other red currants. Harvest the firm red berries in late summer once the whole strig has colored evenly.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; among the sweetest of the white currants.

directions: Set the crown a little below grade and mulch to protect the shallow roots. Thin the oldest canes each winter to keep the low, spreading bush productive. Pick the pale, nearly translucent berries when they turn creamy and sweet in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; an old French white variety, mild and productive.

directions: Firm the roots into rich soil and water in, then keep the bed weed-free and mulched. Prune to an open framework so light ripens the fruit evenly. Harvest the pale yellow-white berries on their long strigs when translucent and sweet in early summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant slightly deep, mulch, and keep the roots cool through the growing season. Prune yearly to remove spent wood and open the center. Harvest the sweet, pale berries in midsummer; the crop resists cracking and holds well on the bush.

planting.note: Plant dormant, burying the crown two inches deep to force new basal shoots.

directions: Set plants deep and cut all shoots to two buds after planting to build a compact bush. This variety stays small yet crops heavily, so support the laden branches. Harvest the large black berries when fully colored and slightly soft in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; a vigorous, upright variety tolerant of cold sites.

directions: Set the plant two inches deeper than it grew before and cut the canes back after planting. Feed and mulch each spring, since black currants are heavy feeders. Pick the large aromatic berries when they blacken fully in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; a compact, early, heavy-yielding modern variety.

directions: Bury the crown to promote basal shoots and prune the young bush to open its center. Its compact habit and large berries make it well suited to small gardens and containers. Harvest the big, glossy black fruit early in the season once fully colored.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; a rust-resistant variety often allowed where currants are restricted.

directions: Bury the crown and cut back hard after planting to build a full bush. This cultivar's resistance to white pine blister rust makes it acceptable in some restricted areas. Harvest the small, richly flavored black berries in midsummer for juice and preserves.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; an early, large-berried heritage variety needing a sheltered spot.

directions: Set plants deep in fertile soil and shelter the early blossom from spring frost. This vigorous, spreading bush rewards generous feeding and mulching. Harvest the large, sweet black berries early in the season once the strigs color fully.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; a compact heritage variety prized for high vitamin C fruit.

directions: Bury the crown to encourage basal shoots and keep the compact bush well fed. Prune out older wood each year to sustain a steady crop. Harvest the strongly flavored black berries in midsummer for cordials, jam, and freezing.

planting.note: Plant dormant and deep; noted as a sweet dessert black currant good for eating fresh.

directions: Set plants deep, mulch, and feed well in spring. This variety's unusually sweet, large berries can be enjoyed straight from the bush. Harvest early in the season as soon as the fruit turns fully black.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a hardy yellow-green Finnish selection with rich flavor.

directions: Set the bush in well-drained soil and open its center each winter to reduce mildew and ease harvest. Keep it evenly watered as the berries swell. Pick the amber-yellow fruit when fully ripe and aromatic in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a nearly thornless, mildew-resistant American hybrid.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and prune lightly, taking advantage of the nearly spineless canes for easy harvest. Keep it fed and mulched for steady crops. Pick the teardrop, wine-red berries when soft and sweet in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a tough, nearly thornless variety for cold prairie regions.

directions: Set plants in ordinary garden soil, as this variety is very adaptable and cold-hardy. Its berries hang on long stems well below the few thorns, making picking simple. Harvest green for cooking or wait until the fruit blushes pink and sweetens.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a reliable American variety valued for dessert-quality fruit.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and prune to an open bush for airflow. This vigorous variety bears heavy crops of flavorful fruit with relatively few thorns. Harvest the red, sweet berries when fully ripe in midsummer for fresh eating.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a classic English culinary variety with large pale berries.

directions: Plant with the crown just below the surface and prune to keep the spreading bush open. Its large, smooth green berries are ideal for cooking and preserves. Pick the fruit green for pies, or let a portion ripen creamy-white in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in rich soil; a large-fruited dessert exhibition variety.

directions: Give this variety fertile soil and steady moisture, as it needs good conditions to size its large berries. Prune to an open goblet and thin the crop for the biggest fruit. Harvest the sweet, yellow-green dessert berries when fully ripe in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a heritage red variety that tolerates heavier soils and some shade.

directions: Plant this vigorous, spiny variety with room to spread and prune to open the crowded center. It performs on richer, heavier soils where other gooseberries struggle. Harvest the hairy, dark red berries when sweet and ripe in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a currant-gooseberry hybrid with dark, richly flavored berries.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and prune to an open framework for airflow and easy picking. This mildew-resistant hybrid carries small, dark red-purple berries with a wine-like flavor. Harvest when the fruit turns deep and soft in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a very cold-hardy North American selection.

directions: Plant in ordinary well-drained soil, where this hardy variety shrugs off cold prairie winters. Prune to open the center and improve airflow against mildew. Harvest the pinkish-red berries when soft and sweet in midsummer.

planting.note: Grow from hardwood cuttings or bare-root plants in early spring; seed needs cold stratification.

directions: Root dormant cuttings or set bare-root plants with the crown just below grade, then prune to an open goblet on a short leg. Keep the roots cool with mulch and thin crowded shoots for airflow. Harvest the spherical, often hairy berries green for cooking or ripe and sweet in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a thornless black currant and gooseberry hybrid.

directions: Give this large, thornless, vigorous bush plenty of room and plant it slightly deep. It resists mildew and rust and needs little pruning beyond removing old wood. Harvest the dark berries when fully black; taste ranges from tart like gooseberry when young to rich like black currant when ripe.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a North American clove-scented currant grown for fruit and fragrance.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil where its spring flowers can perfume the garden with a clove scent. This adaptable shrub tolerates heat and drought better than European currants once established. Harvest the large black berries when fully ripe in summer, planting more than one clone to improve fruit set.

planting.note: Plant in early spring alongside a compatible late-blooming pollinizer such as Honey Bee or Aurora.

directions: Plant this vigorous University of Saskatchewan selection in well-drained soil with a partner variety nearby for good fruit set. Water steadily through the first two seasons while the framework of branches establishes. Its large, firm berries hold on the bush well and ripen a little later than the early haskaps, giving a longer picking window in early summer.

planting.note: Set out in early spring and pair with Aurora, Tundra, or another compatible variety for pollination.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained ground and keep the root zone mulched to conserve moisture. The berries are unusually large and sweet but the skin is tender, so pick gently into shallow containers. Fruit ripens in early summer and drops readily once fully colored, so check the bushes every few days.

planting.note: Plant in early spring with a second variety close by for reliable pollination.

directions: Give this cultivar full sun and rich soil, and mulch the base to protect the shallow roots. Tundra's berries are firmer than most, so they hold up well to hand and machine picking. Harvest the elongated blue fruit in early summer once the interior has turned fully purple.

planting.note: Plant in early spring near another haskap variety for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot with an early-blooming pollinizer within a few yards. This selection is a heavy producer of tangy berries that are excellent cooked or frozen. Fruit ripens in early summer; taste-test before harvesting since the skin colors up before the flesh is fully sweet.

planting.note: Often planted as a pollinizer for Aurora and the Boreal series; set out in early spring.

directions: Plant this tall, vigorous variety in full sun where it can serve as a pollen source for nearby fruiting haskaps. It also carries its own crop of tart berries useful for juice and baking. Harvest in early summer, and prune out the oldest canes every few years to keep new growth productive.

planting.note: A dependable pollinizer for early-blooming varieties; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, upright shrub in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained soil. It blooms early and pollinates other honeyberries while producing tart blue fruit of its own. Pick the berries in early summer and use them for jam, syrup, or freezing, since they are quite sharp fresh.

planting.note: A late-season variety that pairs with Boreal Beast for pollination; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this late-blooming University of Saskatchewan release with a compatible partner such as Boreal Beast for good fruit set. The large berries hold firmly on the plant and resist dropping, which stretches the harvest. Pick in early to mid summer when the fruit is uniformly dark blue inside and out.

planting.note: Plant in early spring with a late pollinizer such as Boreal Beast nearby.

directions: Give this large, upright cultivar room to spread and full sun for the best crop. It is prized for producing some of the biggest berries of any haskap, excellent for fresh eating. Harvest the sweet-tart fruit in early to mid summer once fully colored throughout.

planting.note: Commonly used as the pollinizer for the Boreal Beauty and Boreal Blizzard varieties.

directions: Plant this late-flowering variety within a few yards of the other Boreal haskaps so their bloom times overlap. Its own fruit is firm and flavorful, useful for processing and fresh eating. Prune lightly after harvest in mid summer to keep the bush open and productive.

planting.note: A Polish-bred variety; plant in early spring with a second haskap for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant this Polish selection in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil with a compatible pollen partner. It is a robust, upright grower that yields long, sweet-tart berries suited to fresh use and juice. Harvest in early summer once the fruit softens slightly and colors fully inside.

planting.note: Partially self-fertile, though yields improve with a second variety; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained bed; although Solo can set some fruit on its own, a neighboring variety greatly increases the crop. Keep young plants watered and mulched through establishment. The plump berries ripen in early summer and are sweet enough for fresh snacking straight off the bush.

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or potted shrubs in early spring in moist ground.

directions: Root hardwood cuttings in spring or set out young plants in rich, consistently moist soil. Grow at least two elderberries near each other to improve pollination and fruit set. Broad clusters of creamy flowers appear in early summer and give way to hanging bunches of small dark berries that must be cooked before eating.

planting.note: A classic partner for Adams; plant both in early spring for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant this productive cultivar in rich, moist ground alongside another elderberry variety for the best yields. York produces some of the largest berries of the older selections and ripens slightly later in the season. Pick the heavy fruit clusters when uniformly dark, and cook them for syrup, wine, or jelly.

planting.note: A late-blooming, heavy-bearing selection; plant in early spring with a pollination partner.

directions: Plant this vigorous cultivar in deep, moist soil and cut it to the ground in late winter for strong annual canes if managed intensively. It blooms late and carries very large flower heads that ripen into big fruit clusters. Harvest in late summer to early fall, then cook the berries for syrup or wine.

planting.note: A short, easily managed cultivar; plant in early spring with another variety nearby.

directions: Plant this compact, upright selection in moist soil where its shorter stature makes picking easy. Ranch blooms early and can be grown as an annually coppiced planting for mechanical harvest. Gather the dark berries in mid to late summer and cook them for juice or preserves.

planting.note: A large, vigorous cultivar often used to pollinate Adams; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this tall, robust cultivar in rich, moist soil where it has plenty of room to grow. It is an excellent pollen source for other elderberries and yields large fruit clusters well suited to juice and jelly. Harvest the purple-black berries in late summer and always cook them before eating.

planting.note: A Danish commercial cultivar; plant in early spring with Samyl or another variety for pollination.

directions: Plant this productive European cultivar in deep, moist soil with a compatible pollinizer nearby. It bears heavy crops of small dark berries on large, drooping heads that are easy to strip. Harvest in late summer and cook the fruit for the concentrated juice used in cordials and syrups.

planting.note: A widely grown European commercial variety; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this vigorous Austrian cultivar in rich, moist soil where its large size can be accommodated. It is a mainstay of the European elderberry industry, valued for reliable heavy crops of deeply colored fruit. Harvest the berries in late summer and cook them down for juice, dye, or wine.

planting.note: Grown mainly for its foliage and flowers; plant in early spring in moist soil.

directions: Plant this finely cut, dark-purple-leaved cultivar in full sun to keep the foliage color deep. Pink-flushed flower heads open in early summer and can ripen into dark berries if a compatible elder is nearby, though it is grown chiefly for looks. Prune in late winter to shape the plant and encourage fresh, colorful growth.

planting.note: An ornamental dark-leaved elder; plant in early spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Plant this purple-black-foliaged shrub in full sun for the richest leaf color and best flowering. Lemon-scented pink flowers appear in early summer and may set dark berries where cross-pollination occurs. Cut back hard in late winter to control size and produce vigorous, deeply colored new stems.

planting.note: An ornamental, fruiting cultivar; self-fertile, plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this selection in full sun to partial shade in well-drained soil for both fruit and ornament. It is noted for showy white spring bloom, glossy black berries, and outstanding purple-red fall color. Gather the astringent fruit in late summer for juice or jam, or leave it for the birds.

planting.note: A dwarf, mounding groundcover form; self-fertile, plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this compact, mounded selection in full sun to part shade as a low border or groundcover in tough sites. It offers white spring flowers, small dark berries, and brilliant red fall color on a tidy plant. The fruit is edible but astringent; leave it for wildlife or gather it in late summer for juice.

planting.note: A Polish commercial cultivar bred for heavy fruiting; plant in early spring.

directions: Plant this productive Polish selection in full sun and well-drained soil; it is self-fertile and needs no partner. Galicjanka is grown widely for processing, yielding large crops of deeply pigmented berries high in antioxidants. Harvest the tart fruit in late summer for juice, wine, or drying.

planting.note: Plant this self-fertile selection in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Choose a sunny, well-drained spot and keep the graft or crown at soil level when planting this compact, early-bearing cultivar. Water steadily through the first season, then reduce once established since the roots resent soggy ground. Harvest the sweet-tart red berries as they ripen over a long window from summer to fall.

planting.note: A heavy-fruiting female; pair with a male such as 'Pollmix'.

directions: Set this productive female selection in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun, keeping a compatible male nearby for pollination. It needs little feeding thanks to its root nodules, and over-rich soil reduces fruiting. Harvest the abundant orange berries in autumn, often by cutting whole fruiting branches to work with indoors.

planting.note: A male pollinator grown only to fertilize female sea buckthorn.

directions: Plant this male clone upwind of your fruiting females so wind carries its pollen to them in spring. It produces no berries itself but is essential for a crop, and one male can service several females. Give it the same lean, well-drained, sunny conditions as the rest of the planting.

planting.note: A heavy-cropping ornamental selection for acidic beds.

directions: Plant this reliably productive cultivar in moist, acidic, well-drained soil with light shade in hot climates. It is prized for both its glossy foliage and its heavy autumn crop, so shear lightly after fruiting to keep it dense. Harvest the firm scarlet berries in fall, letting a touch of frost mellow their tartness.

planting.note: A vigorous early-fruiting selection with large berries.

directions: Set this Dutch selection into cool, acidic soil rich in organic matter and keep it evenly moist. It bears comparatively large berries and often begins cropping young, making it a good pollinizing partner for other lingonberries. Gather the bright red fruit in two waves, in midsummer and again in early fall.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings into acidic, consistently moist ground in spring.

directions: You do not need a flooded bog, but you do need very acidic soil (pH near 4.0 to 5.0) that stays constantly moist, so a sanded peat bed works well. The low runners root as they spread and take about three years to cover a bed and fruit heavily. Harvest the firm red berries in fall, when a ripe one bounces if dropped.

planting.note: A high-yielding standard cultivar for home and commercial beds.

directions: Plant this widely grown, disease-tolerant cultivar into an acidic, sand-topped peat bed and keep it wet through the season. Let the vines knit together the first couple of years before expecting a full crop of large, deep-red berries. Pick in autumn once the fruit colors fully and firms up.

planting.note: An early-ripening cultivar well suited to short seasons.

directions: This early variety colors up ahead of most cranberries, a plus where autumn comes fast. Grow it in the same wet, strongly acidic bed as other cranberries and mulch with sand every year or two to encourage rooting. Harvest the dark red berries as soon as they ripen in early to mid fall.

planting.note: Plant in cool, acidic, humus-rich woodland soil; notoriously slow to establish.

directions: This is the prized wild huckleberry of western mountains and is difficult to domesticate, needing cool, acidic, high-organic soil and often a mycorrhizal partner. Give it dappled light and steady moisture, and be patient because plants are very slow to reach bearing size. The intensely flavored purple-black berries ripen in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring with a second compatible cultivar for pollination.

directions: This extremely cold-hardy shrub flowers very early and ripens the first berries of the season, so give it a spot sheltered from frost pockets. Plant at least two different cultivars that bloom together, since a single plant will not set fruit alone. Harvest the elongated blue berries when the inside is fully purple, not just when the skin colors.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and set plants out once nights stay above 50F.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in warm trays and pot up before transplanting into rich soil after frost danger passes. Give the sprawling vines a sturdy cage or trellis, since a single plant can ramble several feet in every direction. Pick the tiny pea-sized fruit once it colors fully and pops sweet-tart from the stem, and expect heavy clusters straight through until frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors under lights and move seedlings outdoors after the soil warms.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the tray around room temperature until sprouts appear in a week or two. Transplant into well-drained ground and support the wiry, indeterminate stems, which reseed freely if fruit is left to drop. Harvest the burgundy currant-sized tomatoes when deeply colored, snacking straight off long trusses through the warm months.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and set out after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Start seed shallowly in warm flats and grow on until roots fill the cell before planting out. Cage the vigorous vines and water evenly to reduce splitting of the delicate skins. Gather the glossy red fruit in loose sprays once each berry softens slightly, and cut whole trusses to bring the harvest in quickly.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant into warm ground after the last frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into a moist warm mix and keep it bright once the first leaves unfold. Move to fertile, well-drained soil and trellis the running stems, which fruit nonstop until cold weather. Pick the sunshine-yellow currants when fully colored and sweet, running your fingers down a truss to gather many at once.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant into warm soil after frost has passed.

directions: Barely cover the tiny seed on a warm moist surface and thin to the strongest seedlings before setting out. The compact bushy plants sprawl low and self-seed readily where fruit is allowed to drop. Harvest the golden berries from the ground once their papery lanterns dry, enjoying a sweet flavor reminiscent of pineapple and tomato.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a container or a sunny bed for a compact fruiting bush.

directions: Set this naturally small selection at nursery depth in a large pot or border, where it fruits young even as a knee-high plant. Trim after the main flush to keep it tidy and encourage repeat cropping. The dark berries ripen a few at a time over a long season, ready to pick when soft and nearly black.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered spot as it prefers mild winters.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile soil in a protected location and prune to control the vigorous, spreading growth. Provide steady moisture while young to support the long developing fruit. The remarkably elongated berries, often two to four inches long, turn deep purple-black and sweet when ready in late spring to summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a small garden space or roomy container.

directions: Set this naturally compact selection in well-drained soil where its short stature suits tight spaces and pots. Prune lightly to shape and it will fruit heavily even as a small bush. The plump dark berries ripen over an extended window in early summer and are best picked when they release with a gentle tug.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the sweetest, largest fruit.

directions: Set this improved selection in average, well-drained soil where it fixes its own nitrogen and needs little feeding. Give it sun and it rewards with larger, sweeter berries than the wild type. Harvest the glossy red fruit when it softens and deepens in color during early summer, tasting best fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant in spring; pairing with another goumi boosts fruit set.

directions: Plant in any reasonably drained soil, as this hardy shrub tolerates poor ground and wind. Prune after fruiting to keep it compact and productive. The small speckled red berries ripen in early summer and are ready when soft, offering a bright sweet-tart flavor.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a different honeyberry variety nearby for pollination.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in cool, moisture-retentive soil and pair with a compatible pollinizer such as another haskap variety. This extremely cold-hardy shrub leafs out and blooms very early, so a sheltered spot helps in frosty springs. The elongated blue berries ripen well before strawberries and are sweetest when fully colored inside and out.

planting.note: Plant in spring alongside another late-blooming haskap for cross-pollination.

directions: Set this vigorous variety in rich, evenly moist soil and provide a genetically different partner nearby to ensure fruit set. It grows larger and yields more than older selections once established over a few seasons. Pick the plump blue-purple berries when the inside flesh turns fully purple, a truer sign of ripeness than skin color alone.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out after the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Start seed in warm trays and transplant the fast-growing plants into rich, well-drained soil after frost. They crop quickly from a single season and can self-seed in mild climates. Harvest only the fully ripe, dull blue-black berries when soft, and cook them for the best flavor rather than eating the tart green fruit.

planting.note: Set out dormant bushes in early spring, or in fall where winters are mild.

directions: Plant in acidic soil around pH 4.5 to 5.5, keeping the crown level with the surrounding ground and mulching heavily with pine bark or sawdust. Water consistently through the growing season and remove flowers the first year to build a strong root system. This dependable midseason bush ripens firm, mildly sweet berries in July.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring while the soil is still cool.

directions: This vigorous, old-line variety is forgiving of soil and makes an excellent pollinizer for other highbush types. Prune out the oldest canes each winter to keep the bush productive and open to light. The small to medium berries ripen late in the season and hold well for jam and baking.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring for reliable rooting.

directions: A cold-hardy midseason favorite that produces large, aromatic berries with a rich, tart-sweet flavor. Keep the soil acidic and evenly moist, and mulch to suppress weeds that compete with its shallow roots. Expect showy pink-tinged flowers in spring followed by heavy clusters ripening in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant; its late bloom helps avoid frost damage.

directions: This is one of the latest-ripening highbush types, extending the harvest well into late summer. Plant in acidic soil with steady moisture and give it a sunny spot for the best fruit set. The firm, light-blue berries are tart until fully ripe, so let them hang a few days after coloring for sweeter flavor.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in acidic, well-drained soil.

directions: Chandler is prized for producing some of the largest berries of any blueberry, often the size of a quarter. Give it rich, acidic soil and consistent water, and thin lightly to channel energy into fruit size. Its long ripening window stretches the fresh harvest over several weeks in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant for best establishment.

directions: An upright, easy-picking variety whose fruit resists cracking and holds firmly on the bush. Plant in acidic soil and mulch to conserve moisture around the shallow root system. The medium, mild berries ripen early to midseason and hang well for a long harvest.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring in acidic soil.

directions: A spreading, open bush that yields large, pale-blue berries with a mild, sweet flavor. Keep the soil consistently moist and acidic, and prune to keep the center airy. Fruit ripens in midseason and resists dropping, making it forgiving of a less frequent picking schedule.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring; among the first to ripen.

directions: As the name suggests, this variety is one of the earliest to ripen, delivering fruit at the very start of blueberry season. Provide acidic soil, steady moisture, and full sun to bring the crop on quickly. The large, firm berries resist cracking and are best picked as soon as they turn deep blue.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in acidic soil.

directions: Darrow produces very large berries with a bright, tangy flavor that intensifies when fully ripe. Keep the pH low and the soil evenly moist, and mulch to protect its shallow roots. Berries ripen mid to late season and are best left on the bush a few extra days for peak sweetness.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; adaptable across a wide zone range.

directions: This interspecific hybrid adapts to both northern and warmer southern gardens and keeps its foliage late into fall. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and water consistently for its high yields of firm, sweet berries. Fruit ripens midseason and holds excellent flavor and shelf life.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring in acidic soil.

directions: Draper is known for firm, crisp berries with excellent flavor and a long shelf life. Give it acidic soil, full sun, and even moisture to support its concentrated, high-quality crop. Berries ripen in midseason in tight clusters that make hand-picking quick.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; one of the last highbush types to ripen.

directions: Aurora extends the season with a very late ripening window, often bearing after most other bushes are finished. Plant in acidic soil with reliable moisture and full sun to ripen the crop before frost. The large, firm berries have a rich flavor and hold well on the bush.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant in acidic soil.

directions: Liberty is a vigorous, upright late-season variety with sweet, aromatic berries. Keep the soil acidic and consistently moist, and prune older canes to maintain productivity. The medium to large fruit ripens over a long window and stores well after picking.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring.

directions: Toro produces large berries in tight clusters that ripen close together for an efficient harvest. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and water evenly through fruit development. Showy pink spring flowers give way to firm, sweet berries in early to midseason.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in acidic soil.

directions: A hardy, productive variety valued for its large, firm, flavorful berries. Give it a sunny site with acidic soil and steady moisture, and mulch to protect the roots. Fruit ripens mid to late season on a strong, upright bush.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; a heritage wild selection.

directions: Rubel is a heritage selection from the wild, prized for small berries that are exceptionally high in antioxidants. Plant in acidic soil and full sun, and expect a rugged, upright bush that tolerates a range of conditions. The intensely flavored fruit ripens midseason and is excellent for baking.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; needs sharply drained acidic soil.

directions: Spartan bears very large, firm berries with a bright, well-balanced flavor early in the season. It is somewhat fussy about drainage, so plant in loose, acidic soil and avoid soggy spots. Pick the fruit once it fully colors and separates cleanly from the stem.

planting.note: Plant in early spring, or in fall across the mild South.

directions: O'Neal is a low-chill southern highbush that fruits early and reliably in warm climates. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter and keep it evenly moist. The sweet, aromatic berries ripen in late spring, well ahead of northern types.

planting.note: Plant in fall or spring; excellent in containers.

directions: This compact, semi-dwarf variety tolerates a wider soil pH than most and thrives in pots. Plant in acidic potting mix or amended ground soil and keep it consistently moist. It is partly self-fertile and produces tangy-sweet berries in midseason on a tidy, attractive bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in warm regions.

directions: Star is a low-chill southern highbush that ripens its whole crop over a short, concentrated window. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and water evenly during fruiting. The large, firm berries have a mild, sweet flavor and ripen in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring along the Gulf and lower South.

directions: Gulfcoast is a low-chill southern highbush adapted to the humid, mild-winter South. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter and keep it consistently moist. Medium, sweet berries ripen early on a spreading, productive bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring; needs a second rabbiteye for pollination.

directions: Tifblue is a long-lived, heat-tolerant rabbiteye and one of the most widely grown in the South. Plant with another rabbiteye variety nearby for cross-pollination, and give it acidic, well-drained soil. It is more drought-tolerant than highbush types, and its light-blue berries ripen late in the season.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Powderblue is a vigorous rabbiteye with dusty, powdery-blue berries that resist cracking. Plant it near Tifblue or another rabbiteye to ensure good fruit set, and provide acidic soil. The firm, sweet berries ripen late and hold well on the bush in hot weather.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with another early rabbiteye.

directions: Climax is an early-ripening rabbiteye whose berries mature together for a concentrated harvest. Plant with another early variety for pollination and give it acidic, well-drained soil. Its upright habit makes picking easy, and the fruit is ready earlier than most rabbiteyes.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Premier is a productive, adaptable rabbiteye that ripens early to midseason with large, flavorful berries. Plant with another rabbiteye variety for cross-pollination and provide acidic soil. It is vigorous and disease-resistant, making it a good choice for home gardens across the South.
